content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

4 min read
Just now

--

How Linux sandboxes processes by controlling what syscalls they are allowed to make
You run:
firefoxQuestion:
Can browser access:
Everything in kernel?Can compromised app suddenly do:
mount()
ptrace()
reboot()Question:
How do browsers, containers, and sandboxes stop dangerous system calls?
How does:
Docker
Chrome
Kuberneteslimit process behavior?
Answer:
seccomp
One of the most important Linux security mechanisms.
And also one of the most misunderstood.
Today we’ll deeply understand:
What seccomp is
Why Linux needed syscall filtering
How seccomp works internally
Strict mode vs Filter mode
BPF (without scary jargon)
Real-world examples (Docker/Chrome)
Hands-on labs in C
Debugging blocked syscalls
Security mindset behind syscall sandboxing
And yes —
We’ll keep it beginner-friendly.
Let’s begin.

First: The Big Security Problem
Imagine:
Attacker exploits:
Browser vulnerabilityNow attacker controls process.
Question:
What stops attacker from doing:
fork()
mount()
ptrace()
execve()
kill()to compromise system?
DAC?
No.
MAC?
Helps.
But process still has:
Access to kernel through syscalls.
Huge attack surface.
Need stronger restriction.
Linux says:
Fine.You can run.
But only allowed syscalls.
That mechanism is:
seccomp

What Is seccomp?
Simple definition:
seccomp restricts which system calls a process can make.
Key idea:
Linux says:
You are only allowed
certain kernel interactionsEverything else:
Blocked
Killed
Denied
Loggeddepending on policy.
Huge concept.

Real Life Analogy
Imagine airport.
Everyone wants entry.
Security says:
Allowed:
passport check
boardingDenied:
cockpit access
restricted zones
Not everyone gets full access.
Same idea.
seccomp says:
App only needs few syscallsWhy allow:
Dangerous extras?
What Is a Syscall?
Quick refresher
Programs cannot directly access:
CPU hardware
Files
Memory management
Kernel internalsInstead:
They ask kernel.
Example:
open()
read()
write()
fork()
execve()These are:
System calls
Huge realization:
Everything meaningful in Linux eventually becomes a syscall.

Example
When you do:
cat file.txtUnderneath:
Linux executes:
open()
read()
write()
close()Question:
What if we block:
open()?
Program breaks.
Interesting.

Why seccomp Exists
Question:
Why give process:
300+ syscallswhen it only needs:
20?Example:
Browser tab.
Needs:
read()
write()
recv()
send()
mmap()Does NOT need:
reboot()
mount()
ptrace()Huge security insight:
Less attack surface = safer system
This is:
Principle of least privilege.

Two Types of seccomp
Linux supports:
1. Strict Mode
Very restrictive.
Only allows:
read()
write()
exit()
sigreturn()Everything else:
KilledVery old mode.
Rarely used.

2. Filter Mode (Modern)
Powerful.
You decide:
Allow this syscall
Block that syscallMost real systems use:
seccomp-BPF
Huge concept.

What Is seccomp-BPF?
Sounds terrifying.
Reality:
Simple.
Linux uses:
Tiny filtering program
to decide:
Allow syscall?
Deny syscall?Think:
Firewall.
But for:
System callsInstead of network packets.
Huge mental model.

Real World Usage
Chrome
Browser tabs:
SandboxedCompromised tab:
Cannot suddenly:
Take over OS
Docker
Containers get:
Default seccomp profile.
Blocks dangerous syscalls.
Example:
ptrace
reboot
kexec_load
mountHuge container security layer.

Kubernetes
Pods inherit:
seccomp profiles.
Enterprise security.

Hands-On Lab 1 — Observe Syscalls
Install:
sudo apt install straceRun:
strace lsMind blown moment.
You’ll see:
openat()
read()
mmap()
close()
write()Linux internals exposed.
You’re literally watching:
Kernel conversations.

First seccomp Program
Create:
// strict.c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/prctl.h>
#include <linux/seccomp.h>
int main()
{
printf("Before seccomp\n");
prctl(PR_SET_SECCOMP,
SECCOMP_MODE_STRICT);
printf("After seccomp\n");
return 0;
}Compile:
gcc strict.c -o strictRun:
./strictInteresting.
Program survives.
Why?
Because:
printf()mostly becomes:
write()Allowed syscall.

Hands-On Lab 2 — Trigger seccomp Kill
Modify:
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/prctl.h>
#include <linux/seccomp.h>int main()
{
prctl(PR_SET_SECCOMP,
SECCOMP_MODE_STRICT);
fork();
return 0;
}
Compile:
gcc strict.c -o strictRun:
./strictExpected:
Bad system callor:
KilledHuge realization:
seccomp blocked fork()
Because strict mode only allows:
read
write
exit
sigreturnMind blown?

Hands-On Lab 3 — Observe With strace
Run:
strace ./strictInteresting.
You’ll literally see:
+++ killed by SIGKILL +++Meaning:
Illegal syscallHuge Linux security moment.

What Happens Internally?
Process does:
fork()Kernel checks:
seccomp policyPolicy says:
DeniedKernel action:
Kill processVisual:
Process
↓ syscall
Kernel
↓ seccomp filter
ALLOW / DENYSimple.
Beautiful.

Hands-On Lab 4 — Docker seccomp
Check Docker profile:
docker info | grep seccompRun container:
docker run -it ubuntu bashCheck:
grep Seccomp /proc/self/statusExample:
Seccomp: 2Meaning:
Filter mode activeHuge realization:
Containers are sandboxed.

seccomp Actions
Modern seccomp can:
Allow
Proceed
Kill
Terminate process
Errno
Return:
Permission denied
Log
Record violation.

Trap
Notify debugger.
Very powerful.

Hands-On Lab 5 — Observe seccomp Status
Run:
grep Seccomp /proc/self/statusExample:
Seccomp: 0Means:
No seccompInside container:
May see:
Seccomp: 2Meaning:
Filtering activeInteresting Linux insight.

Why seccomp Matters
Without seccomp:
Compromised process can try:
Privilege escalation
Kernel abuse
Dangerous syscallsWith seccomp:
Linux says:
You don't need that syscall.
Denied.Huge attack reduction.

seccomp vs AppArmor vs SELinux
Very important.
AppArmor / SELinux
Control:
Files
Permissions
Capabilities
Resources
seccomp
Controls:
Kernel syscallsDifferent layer.
Visual:
Process
↓
DAC
↓
MAC
↓
seccomp
↓
Kernel syscall accessBeautiful layered defense.

Common Beginner Misconceptions
1. “seccomp is firewall”
No.
Firewall for:
Syscallsnot network.

2. “seccomp replaces AppArmor”
No.
Different security layers.

3. “Process can bypass seccomp”
No.
Kernel enforced.
Huge difference.

4. “Containers are secure by default”
Safer.
Not invincible.
seccomp helps.

Mini Challenge
Question 1:
What does:
seccomp
restrict?

Question 2:
Why does:
fork()fail in strict mode?

Question 3:
What is:
seccomp-BPF?

Question 4:
Why do containers love seccomp?
Think deeply.

The Big Mental Model
Linux security:
Process
↓
Syscall request
↓
seccomp policy
↓
ALLOW / BLOCK
↓
KernelBeautiful engineering.

Final Thoughts
At first:
System calls feel invisible.
Programs just:
WorkFeels magical.
But underneath?
Linux is constantly asking:
Should this syscall happen?
Does process really need it?
Is this dangerous?And once this clicks…
You stop seeing Linux security as magic.
You finally understand:
How Linux sandboxes processes using seccomp.
